summary: Gruber previews WWDC 2011, admitting he knows very little about the big announcements but sharing his best guess: iCloud should be understood not as a replacement for MobileMe, but as the new iTunes — a cloud-based canonical store that replaces USB tethering between iOS devices and desktop iTunes. He argues this shift would transform the iOS out-of-box experience and potentially turn Apple TV into a movie-buying platform. The second half covers his iOS wishlist: Exposé-style app switching, persistent split views in iPad portrait mode, and a fundamental rethink of homescreen app organization.
key_points: iCloud is best understood as the new iTunes, not the new MobileMe — it shifts the canonical data store from a desktop app to the cloud
Cloud syncing would eliminate the clunky requirement to plug new iOS devices into a Mac/PC before first use
iCloud will likely cannibalize MobileMe's value proposition as a side effect, since most of MobileMe's selling points overlap with iCloud's features
iOS has added extensively since 2007 but changed very little of its existing UI, and the app switcher, iPad Mail layout, and homescreen organization all need rethinking
Apple's iOS 4 app switcher feels like another launcher rather than a true switcher, and an Exposé-style thumbnail UI would better serve the task
money_quotes: Don't think of iCloud as the new MobileMe; think of iCloud as the new iTunes.
Music storage is a feature of iCloud; iCloud is not a music service.
Syncing data between devices tends to work best when there's a canonical store.
iCloud may well obviate much of MobileMe simply as a side effect.
One thing Apple hasn't done much of with iOS so far is change anything. They've added a lot since the iPhone debuted in 2007, but they haven't changed much.
What Apple shipped in iOS 4 feels a bit more like another launcher, where apps are ordered by most-recently-used, rather than a switcher.
I can't help but wish for a homescreen revamp that's designed from the ground up for a platform where users have dozens of apps installed, and are adding new ones all the time.
key_insight: iCloud's real significance is not as a music locker or MobileMe replacement, but as the cloud-based canonical store that finally cuts the cord between iOS devices and desktop iTunes.
